sanskrit_devanagari:
प्रकृतेर्गुणसम्मूढाः सज्जन्ते गुणकर्मसु ।
तानकृत्स्नविदो मन्दान्कृत्स्नविन्न विचालयेत् ॥ २९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
prakṛter guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ
sajjante guṇa-karmasu |
tān akṛtsna-vido mandān
kṛtsna-vin na vicālayet || 29 ||

anvaya.meaning: persons with a poor fund of knowledge
one who is in factual knowledge

translations.verbatim_quote: Bewildered by the modes of material nature, the ignorant fully engage themselves in material activities and become attached. But the wise should not unsettle them, although these duties are inferior due to the performers’ lack of knowledge.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Persons who are unknowledgeable falsely identify with gross material consciousness and are full of material designations. This body is a gift of the material nature, and one who is too much attached to the bodily consciousness is called manda, or a lazy person without understanding of spirit soul.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.29 Those again, guna-sammudhah, who are wholly deluded by the gunas; prakrteh, of Nature; sajjante, become attached; guna karmasu, to the activities of the gunas, thining, ‘We do actions for results.’ Krtsna-vit, the knower of the All, one who is himself a knower of the Self; na
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: prakṛter guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ — those deluded by the gunas of prakṛti — sajjante guṇa-karmasu — are attached to the actions of the gunas. tān akṛtsna-vidaḥ mandān — these of partial-knowledge, dull — kṛtsna-vit na vicālayet — the knower of the whole should not unsettle. The verse explicitly identifies the loka-saṅgraha rule of 3.26 with the metaphysical claim of 3.27: the partial-knower's attachment to action is the proper modality for his state; destabilising him produces drift, not progress.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.29 Those who ‘do not know the whole truth’ are those persons who are trying for the vision of the self but are deluded about the nature of the self, not knowing, on account of their involvement in Prakrti, that actions proceed from the Gunas of Prakrti.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: those deluded by the gunas — guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ — are attached to the actions of the gunas, treating themselves as the agents. The kṛtsna-vit, who has fuller knowledge of the gunas' operation, should not unsettle these akṛtsna-vidaḥ — partial-knowers — by destabilising their attachment to their own karma. The mechanism is the same as 3.26: premature unsettling leaves the partial-knower with neither his own karma nor the realisation that exempts from it.

positions.rendering: those who are bewildered by the modes of material nature engage themselves fully in material activities and become attached. But the wise should not unsettle them, although these duties are inferior due to the performers' lack of knowledge
those who are deluded by the modes of nature get attached to the actions performed by them. But the wise, who understand these truths, should not unsettle the ignorant who know less

the kṛtsna-vit (one who knows the totality — the Self as distinct from the gunas) should not, by speech or action, generate doubt in the akṛtsna-vidaḥ (those who know only the part), because such doubt would lead them to abandon the karma that is, in their state, their proper path

the one who knows the gunas in their fullness (kṛtsna) should not destabilise the one who has only partial knowledge of how the gunas operate; the partial-knower's path is to perform their karma; destabilising them produces drift, not progress

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators read the verse as the loka-saṅgraha rule restated in the metaphysical language Krishna has just introduced (3.27-3.28). The kṛtsna-vit / akṛtsna-vidaḥ distinction parallels the vidvān / avidvāṁsaḥ distinction of 3.25-3.26 but specifically in terms of knowledge of the gunas. Translators preserve the surface but lose the structural parallel.

literal_meaning: Those deluded by the gunas of prakṛti are attached to the actions of the gunas; the knower-of-the-whole (kṛtsna-vit) should not unsettle these of incomplete knowledge (akṛtsna-vidaḥ), the dull (mandān).
traditional_meaning_consensus: Closely paired with 3.26, restated in the metaphysical language of 3.27-3.28. The fully-knowing should not unsettle (na vicālayet) those who are guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ (deluded by the gunas) and only akṛtsna-vidaḥ (knowers of part, not whole). The verse extends the loka-saṅgraha implementation rule from 3.26 by giving its metaphysical justification.
